title: One Sheet of Plywood Hall Tree
summary: An entryway bench with cubby storage and an upper hutch, cut entirely from one sheet of plywood plus a little trim — a genuine exercise in efficient sheet-good layout.
description:
The entire piece — bench, cubbies, and upper hutch — comes from a single sheet of 3/4" plywood, which means the very first step is really a layout puzzle: rip the sheet into long strips first, then cross-cut those strips into the individual parts. Plan the whole cut sequence before touching the saw, since you can't mark every cut ahead of time — the blade's kerf eats real material, and marking piece two based on where piece one's edge *should* be rather than where it actually landed compounds error across the sheet.

Casters on the base turn this from a fixed bench into something that can be pulled out for cleaning behind it. The hutch shelf pins are worth a specific callback: attach a 1x3 to the back underside of each shelf as a mounting rail for hooks, so that when a shelf gets repositioned up or down, its hooks move with it instead of staying fixed to the case at the wrong height.

tools.note: A guide or straightedge helps rip long, accurate strips from the sheet without a table saw.

cutList.note: Should be 33" long — cut to fit your actual hutch opening.
Attached to the back underside of each shelf so hooks move with the shelf when repositioned.

steps.title: Break down the plywood sheet
steps.body:
Rip two strips 8 ft long by 15-3/4" wide. Cross-cut 38" off one of those strips. Rip the remaining strip and the 38" piece into 7-3/4"-wide strips for the hutch parts. If you don't have a table saw, use a straightedge or guide with a circular saw for accurate rip cuts.

Plan the full cut sequence before starting — you can't mark every cut ahead of time, since the blade's kerf consumes real material with each pass.

steps.title: Assemble the base storage compartments
steps.body: Lay out the bench box from the 48" x 15-3/4" bench top, the 46-1/2" x 15-3/4" bench back, and the two 14" cubby dividers standing as the outer ends. The back at 46-1/2" is exactly two 3/4" thicknesses less than the 48" top, so the back fits BETWEEN the two outer verticals under a top that runs the full 48" width — get that relationship right and every later part lands; get it backwards and the bench comes out 1-1/2" wrong in a way you won't spot until the back panel refuses to fit. Mark every joint with a square before drilling. Drill 3/4" pocket holes and assemble with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, siting the holes on faces that end up hidden inside a cubby or under the bench, and check the diagonals match before the last screws go home.

steps.title: Add the cubby dividers
steps.body: Mark the two 10-3/4" low dividers' positions across the 46-1/2" span, dividing it into cubbies, and square each line across both the top and the back so the divider stands plumb rather than leaning. Use the arithmetic as your check: 10-3/4" plus the 2-1/2" footer plus the 3/4" top is exactly the 14" of the outer dividers, so the low ones are meant to land on the footer rather than the floor — dry-fit one against the footer before you drill anything, because the plan doesn't state this outright. Attach these with countersunk screws driven in from the outside rather than pocket holes, so nothing shows on the inside face of an open cubby. Fill and sand the countersinks before you finish.

steps.title: Attach the footer
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the top edge and both ends of the three 46-1/2" 1x3 footer pieces. Fit the first across the bench front, set back slightly from the front edge — the small reveal reads as a deliberate recess where a flush joint that misses by a hair just reads as sloppy. The plan supplies three footers but only places the front one, so put the remaining two where the casters and the bench trim need solid backing beneath the box. Each 46-1/2" footer takes one of the 4 ft 1x3s, and the fourth board yields all three 15" hook mounting rails. Attach with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws, or with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue if you'd rather not drill.

steps.title: Add the bench trim
steps.body: Attach the remaining trim boards under the bench with 1-1/4" brad nails and wood glue.

steps.title: Build the upper hutch
steps.body: Build the hutch case from the two 36-1/4" sides and the 46-1/2" x 7-3/4" top, using 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws. At 7-3/4" deep against the bench's 15-3/4", the hutch is deliberately much shallower — that setback is what keeps coats and hooks clear of the back of anyone sitting on the bench. Assemble it flat on the floor and check it square across the diagonals before anything is permanent; the 1/4" back panel goes on later and will lock in whatever shape the case is in at that moment, square or not.

steps.title: Attach the hutch dividers
steps.body: Set the two 7-3/4"-wide hutch dividers vertically inside the case, dividing its width into bays. They are listed at 33", which is the 36-1/4" side less the 2-1/2" back header and the 3/4" top — but measure your actual opening and cut to that number, because any error accumulated in the case shows up here first. Attach with countersunk screws through the case, checking each divider is plumb before the second screw goes in. The hutch shelves ride on pins in these dividers, so a divider that leans gives you a shelf that rocks on its pins forever after.

steps.title: Add the back header
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the ends and the bottom edge of the 46-1/2" x 2-1/2" back header, and fit it across the back of the hutch the same way the bench footer went on. At 46-1/2" it matches the hutch top and the bench back exactly, so it sits between the hutch sides rather than lapping over them. It stiffens the open back of a tall shallow case and gives the 1/4" back panel something solid to nail into along its top edge.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Glue and nail the 1/4" plywood back panel across the back of the piece with 1-1/4" brad nails, driving into the bench back, the hutch sides, the dividers and the back header. Square the case up to the panel's factory corner before the first nails go in — a thin back is what actually holds a tall, shallow case square once it is standing up. Note the panel is 48" x 48": that matches the 48" width, but the 14" bench stacked with the 36-1/4" hutch is 50-1/4" tall, so it will not cover the full height. Decide before nailing where that 2-1/4" gap falls, bearing in mind the bench already has its own 3/4" back closing that part of the box. Mark the internal divider positions on the outside face of the panel first so you aren't nailing blind into air.

steps.title: Install shelves, hooks, and casters
steps.body:
Mount the hutch shelves on shelf pins for adjustability. Attach a 1x3 hook mounting rail to the back underside of each shelf so hooks travel with the shelf if it's ever repositioned. Add hooks to the rails.

Attach the caster wheels to the base of the bench.

images.alt: One sheet of plywood hall tree with bench, cubbies, and hutch
